text:

hip pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
2001-12-13
Pelvic EUA


History of Present Illness:
17 yo F transferred from Telecare Heritage Psychiatric Health Facility Hospital with multiple pelvic
fractures after
MVC car vs pole at mod to high speed. No LOC. Pt restrained. Pt
transferred here for evaluation of fractures. No ETOH.


Past Medical History:
none

Denies ETOH and tobacco
[**Male First Name (un) **] in High School
Lives with grandmother on 01326 Victoria Mountain Apt. 123
East Thomasstad, VI 56178-7778.  Both parents alive and
involved.

Family History:
non contributory

hands and face
                        Neuro: Speech fluent

MCH-27.7 MCHC-32.8 RDW-12.5
1981-12-26 11:15AM   NEUTS-91.7* LYMPHS-5.6* MONOS-2.4 EOS-0.1
BASOS-0.3
1981-12-26 11:15AM   PLT COUNT-290
1981-12-26 11:15AM   GLUCOSE-102 UREA N-12 CREAT-1.3* SODIUM-142
POTASSIUM-4.3 CHLORIDE-111* TOTAL CO2-22 ANION GAP-13
1981-12-26 10:42PM   HCT-29.4*

1981-12-26 CT Pelvis : There is a left inferior pubic ramus
fracture. There are bilateral superior pubic rami fractures. In
addition, there is comminuted fracture of the left sacrum
extending into the left SI joint (3, 13). No other fractures are
identified.
  IMPRESSION:
1. Large amount of contrast identified outside the bladder lumen
consistent with extraperitoneal bladder rupture into the
retropubic space, and tracking superiorly along fascial planes.
Fluid is also seen in the retroperitoneum at the common iliac
vessels, and in the presacral space. Amount fluid present has
increased since the outside comparison CT from Telecare Heritage Psychiatric Health Facility Hospital
from 6 hours previously.
2. Presacral fluid with hyperdensity. While this likely
represents urine
contrast from bladder rupture (due to the small amount of
accumulated fluid since the injury), active extravasation cannot
be excluded in theabsence of precontrast or delayed imaging.
Targetted delayed imaging may be useful in further
differentiating these if of clinical significance.
3. Pelvic fractures as described.



Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. Jackson was evaluated by the Trauma team in the
Emergency Room and then admitted to the Trauma floor for further
evaluation and management.  Her pain was controlled with a
Dilaudid PCA and she was kept NPO.  She was seen by the
Orthopedic service to assess her multiple pelvic fractures and
sacral fracture.  An examination under anesthesia was done on
2001-12-13 which showed a stable sacrum therefore she would be
treated non operatively.

The Urology service was also consulted regarding her bladder
rupture that was noted on CT scan.   They confirmed the finding
and treatment was for long term Foley catheter placement for at
least 3 weeks to keep the bladder decompressed and to give it a
chance to heal.  She will have a cystogram in 3 weeks with
possible catheter removal based on the study.

She was seen by the Physical Therapy service for transfer
training and balance on a daily basis but unfortunately did not
progress enough to transfer safely with one assist and therefore
was referred for a short term rehab prior to her return home.
She will continue Lovenox for 4 weeks for DVT prophylaxis and
will need follow up with the Orthopedic and Urology services
here as stated in the Discharge Planning section.


Medications on Admission:
none

Discharge Medications:
1. Docusate Sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2
times a day).
Disp:*60 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2*
2. Enoxaparin 40 mg/0.4 mL Syringe Sig: Forty (40) mg
Subcutaneous DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 mg* Refills:*0*
3. Tramadol 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO QID (4 times a
day).
4. Morphine 15 mg Tablet Sig: 1-2 Tablets PO Q4H (every 4 hours)
as needed for pain.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Wheaton Franciscan Healthcare - Elmbrook Memorial Cape & Islands

Discharge Diagnosis:
S/P MVC
1. Right presacral hematoma
2. B/L pubic rami fracture
3. Left sacral fracture at S1
4. Bladder rupture
5. Acute blood loss anemia


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status:Clear and coherent
Level of Consciousness:Alert and interactive
Activity Status:Out of Bed with assistance to chair or
wheelchair


Discharge Instructions:
* Your car accident caused multiple injuries in your pelvis
including broken bones, a collection of blood and partial
rupture of your bladder.
* Keep the urinary catheter in place for at least 3 weeks
(1993-02-13). In the interim call the Minnesota Valley Hlth Ctr Inc Clinic at
931-398-4706 for an appointment(see below)  Make sure that the
catheter is patent and draining. Wash your perineum daily with
soap and water.
* Follow the Physical Therapy recommendations to gradually
increase your activity.
* Take enough pain medication so that you are comfortable.
* Drink alot of liquids and eat alot of fiber to avoid
constipation as the narcotic pain medications can be very
constipating.  A stool softener will also help if taken on a
regular basis.
* Call us if you have any questions, concerns or new problems.

Followup Instructions:
Call Dr. Scott at 591-760-6753 for a follow up appointment
in 4 weeks. You will need pelvis films done at that time.

Call the Minnesota Valley Hlth Ctr Inc Clinic at 931-398-4706 for a follow up
appointment in 2 weeks with a cystogram.  The secretary can book
that for you.



Completed by:1993-09-25
